planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Chilean jasmine is more cold-hardy than the commonly sold tropical Mandevilla cultivars — surviving to zone 7b with root mulching, where it dies back to the ground and regrows. It blooms in summer, producing clusters of large, pure-white, funnel-shaped, intensely fragrant flowers. All parts are milky-sapped and toxic if ingested. Provide a sturdy climbing structure.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Chilean jasmine is the most cold-hardy and most fragrant of the mandevilla vines — significantly more cold-tolerant than the showy tropical Mandevilla sanderi hybrids commonly sold in summer, surviving reliably to zone 7b and making it a viable perennial vine for gardens far colder than those where tropical mandevillas must be overwintered indoors. The flowers are produced in summer in loose clusters of 3–5, each flower a large (2–3 inch), pure-white, funnel-shaped, five-petaled blossom with a slightly twisted, windmill-like arrangement of the petals and a powerfully sweet, intense fragrance that is often compared to jasmine but with a richer, more complex depth than most jasmines — one of the most fragrant summer-climbing vines available for temperate gardens. It twines vigorously and can reach 15–20 feet on a sturdy support over several seasons. In zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens it performs as an outstanding, reliable perennial climber with an extended summer bloom season. The white flowers and sweet fragrance make it ideal for placement near porches, pergolas, and garden seating areas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; herald's trumpet is a very large, vigorous vine requiring substantial, permanent support — a mature plant can cover a large pergola or building wall 40–50 feet across. It blooms in spring (April–June), producing large clusters of enormous, white, fragrant, trumpet-shaped flowers. In zone 9 it may be frost-damaged in hard winters but regrows from the roots. Requires consistent moisture during the growing season.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, moderately to richly fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; herald's trumpet is one of the most spectacular of all tropical and subtropical flowering vines — a native of the eastern Himalayan foothills and northern India that produces in spring (April through June) enormous, trumpet-shaped flowers of breathtaking scale: each individual flower is 4–5 inches long and 3–4 inches across at the opening, pure white, five-petaled, with a narrow tube flaring to a wide, rounded, slightly fragrant trumpet that resembles an Easter lily flower of exaggerated dimensions. These enormous white trumpet flowers are borne in large, clustered bunches of 6–20 at the ends of the stems, so that a mature vine in full spring bloom — covering a large pergola or two-story wall — presents hundreds of the enormous, pristine-white trumpets simultaneously in a display of singular tropical grandeur. The large, oval, glossy, dark-green leaves are bold and handsome year-round. The vine is very large-growing, reaching 30–50 feet on suitable permanent support, and is a decades-long commitment. In zone 9a it requires a warm, sheltered position and may suffer frost damage in severe winters but regrows. One of the ultimate ambitions of warm-climate gardeners — a vine you plant and tend for years to achieve an unforgettable eventual spectacle.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sydney golden wattle is extremely fast-growing — potentially reaching 20 feet in 3–5 years — and is used as a quick-growing screen or windbreak plant. It blooms in winter and spring (July–October in Australia; January–April in zone 9). More coastal and moisture-tolerant than many wattles. Listed as invasive in parts of the Mediterranean region, Portugal, and South Africa — use with awareness in mild climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sydney golden wattle is one of Australia's most fast-growing, most fragrant, and most adaptable coastal wattles — native to the sandy coastal heaths, dune systems, and dry sclerophyll forests along the entire Australian east coast from Queensland to Victoria. Unlike the finely-divided, feathery bipinnate leaves of A. baileyana or A. dealbata, Sydney golden wattle is a phyllode-bearing species with long, narrow, lance-shaped, bright-green phyllodes that create a dense, willow-like screen of dark-green foliage year-round. In winter and spring, the branches are covered in dense, cylindrical spikes of small, bright-golden-yellow flowers (rather than spherical pompoms) with a powerful, sweet, characteristic wattle fragrance. The cylindrical spike form (rather than pompom) is immediately distinguishing. Its fast growth — reaching 15–20 feet in only a few years — makes it valuable as a quick-growing, fragrant, flowering screen or windbreak in coastal zone 9 gardens. More tolerant of coastal conditions, sandy soils, and occasional moisture than the inland species. The outstanding fragrance and very fast growth make it a practical choice for rapid effect in new gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs with the upper half exposed above the soil surface in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; spotted crossyne flowers in late summer and autumn (February–April in South Africa) before the leaves emerge (hysteranthous). Keep dry in summer. The species name 'guttata' means spotted — the individual flowers are white to pale pink and often marked with darker spots or streaks. A rare collector's bulb native to the fynbos of the Northern Cape.

directions: Plant bulbs (upper half exposed) in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; spotted crossyne is a rare, collectible South African bulb in the Amaryllidaceae family — related to Brunsvigia and Boophone — from the arid, rocky, fynbos and Namaqualand landscapes of the Northern Cape. Like Brunsvigia and Boophone it flowers in late summer and autumn on a leafless scape that emerges before the leaves (hysteranthous), producing a rounded, somewhat hemispherical umbel of 20–40 individual, tubular-funnel, white to pale pink flowers that are frequently spotted or streaked with darker markings — the 'guttata' (spotted) character. The whole flower head has a somewhat informal, airy quality compared to the more spectacular Brunsvigia josephinae or the bizarre Boophone disticha, but it provides the authentic autumn bloom timing and the spotted flower pattern that make it of botanical interest for the collector of South African autumn-blooming amaryllids. After the autumn flowers, the strap-shaped, grey-green leaves emerge and persist through winter and spring. A specialist plant for the dedicated Cape bulb collector; not widely available in commerce.

planting.note: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; thick-stemmed pelargonium grows from a stout, woody, succulent trunk base that stores water and gives the plant a caudex-like appearance similar to a small succulent tree. It follows the Cape winter-rainfall cycle — growing and flowering in winter and spring, going summer dormant. Keep dry in summer. The swollen, elephant-leg-like trunk base is the primary ornamental feature of mature plants.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; thick-stemmed pelargonium is one of the more unusual succulent pelargoniums from the arid, rocky environments of Namaqualand and the Northern Cape — developing over years a swollen, fleshy, woody, bulbous trunk base of increasing size and eccentricity, from which emerge the annual stems bearing finely divided, somewhat hairy, green leaves and small clusters of white to cream-white pelargonium flowers with typical geranium-family two-upper/three-lower petal arrangement. The swollen, water-storing trunk base — sometimes described as 'elephant-foot' or 'caudex-like' — is the primary ornamental distinction, giving mature plants the appearance of tiny, gnarled, succulent trees. In the arid, rocky Namaqualand environment it is an adaptation to water storage through the long, summer drought periods. For collectors of succulent plants and unusual geranium-family species, the novelty of a pelargonium developing into a swollen-trunked, bonsai-like succulent miniature tree over many years makes it a rewarding long-term specimen. The genus Pelargonium includes many such succulent-stem species from the arid Cape, of which P. carnosum is one of the more robust and accessible.
